attributions.name: Edouard Machery
attributions.affiliation: University of Pittsburgh, Department of History and Philosophy of Science

content.text: Experimental philosophy, broadly understood, is the empirical study of empirical questions that are philosophically significant. More narrowly understood, it is the empirical study of philosophically significant concepts, such as the concepts of knowledge or causation, and judgments, such as the so-called Gettier intuition (the judgment that one does not acquire knowledge if one forms a true judgment by luck). While the methods of experimental philosophy were originally drawn from experimental psychology, experimental philosophers have recently broadened their toolbox by including qualitative interviews, ethnographic methods, text analysis, and citation analysis. The scope of experimental philosophy has also been broadened in recent years, and it has transformed itself into a thriving interdisciplinary endeavor.
Philosophers have engaged in scientific and sometimes empirical inquiry for centuries: Aristotle reported biological observations (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Lennox, J. G. (2001). Aristotle’s philosophy of biology: Studies in the origins of life science. Cambridge University Press.
Descartes, R. (1998). Descartes: The world and other writings (S. Gaukroger, Ed.). Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/CBO9780511605727

content.text: ), Kant wrote treaties about geography and cosmology (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Schönfeld, M., & Thompson, M. (2023). Kant’s philosophical development. In E. N. Zalta and U. Nodelman (Eds.), The Stanford encyclopedia of philosophy (Winter 2023 Edition). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/win2023/entries/kant-development/

content.text: ), and James was a psychologist and philosopher (

attrs.unstructuredValue: James, W. (2007). The principles of psychology. (Original work published 1890). Cosimo Classics.

content.text: ). Although their philosophical ideas were rarely disconnected from their scientific research, it is, however, not always clear whether philosophers viewed their scientific work as means to answer philosophical questions (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Sytsma, J., & Livengood, J. (2019). On experimental philosophy and the history of philosophy: A reply to Sorell. British Journal for the History of Philosophy, 27(3), 635–647. https://doi.org/10.1080/09608788.2018.1539946

content.text: The first attempts to conduct explicitly empirical work to address philosophical questions can be traced to Naess’s (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Naess, A. (1938a). Common sense and truth. Theoria: A Swedish Journal of Philosophy, 4, 39–58.

Naess, A. (1938b). “Truth” as conceived by those who are not professional philosophers (Skrifter Utgitt av Det Norske Videnskaps-Akademi I Oslo Il. Hist.- Filos. Klass 1938 No. 4). I Komisjon Hos Jacob Dybwad.

content.text: ) empirical study of the concept of truth (for historical discussion, see

attrs.unstructuredValue: Barnard, R., & Ulatowski, J. (2016). Tarski’s 1944 polemical remarks and Naess’ “experimental philosophy.” Erkenntnis, 81, 457–477. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10670-015-9750-1
Brandt, R. B. (1954). Hopi ethics: A theoretical analysis. University of Chicago Press.

content.text: anthropological research on moral judgments among the Hopi. Brandt’s goal was to examine, by ethnographic means, whether variation in moral judgment (e.g., the judgment that it is not morally wrong to torture animals for fun) could be explained by appealing to differences in empirical beliefs (e.g., whether or not animals feel pain); his empirical interest was prompted by the so-called “argument from disagreement” in meta-ethics: If moral judgments vary without variation in empirical beliefs, then moral judgments are implausibly viewed as describing an objective moral reality (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Doris, J., Stich, S., Phillips, J., & Walmsley, J. (2020). Moral psychology: Empirical approaches. In E. N. Zalta (Ed.), The Stanford encyclopedia of philosophy (Spring 2020 Edition). https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/spr2020/entries/moral-psych-emp/

content.text: Despite these precursors, experimental philosophy emerged only in the early 2000s as a self-aware research program in philosophy, with a series of influential articles (e.g.,

attrs.unstructuredValue: Knobe, J. (2003). Intentional action and side effects in ordinary language. Analysis, 63(3), 190–194. https://doi.org/10.1093/analys/63.3.190
Machery, E., Mallon, R., Nichols, S., & Stich, S. P. (2004). Semantics, cross-cultural style. Cognition, 92(3), B1–B12. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2003.10.003

Nahmias, E., Morris, S., Nadelhoffer, T., & Turner, J. (2005). Surveying freedom: Folk intuitions about free will and moral responsibility. Philosophical Psychology, 18(5), 561–584. https://doi.org/10.1080/09515080500264180
Weinberg, J. M., Nichols, S., & Stich, S. (2001). Normativity and epistemic intuitions. Philosophical Topics, 29(1/2), 429–460. https://doi.org/10.5840/philtopics2001291/217
Knobe, J., & Nichols, S. (Eds.). (2008). Experimental philosophy. Oxford University Press. https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780195323252.001.0001

content.text: ). Experimental philosophy was originally a topic of heated controversy in philosophy, since it challenged the dominant methods in philosophy, which left no room for empirical work (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Williamson T. (2007). The philosophy of philosophy. Blackwell. https://doi.org/10.1002/9780470696675

content.text: ). After a decade of controversy, experimental philosophy has gone mainstream within the field, with articles on a broad range of topics, using different methods, published in prominent philosophy outlets.
It is common to distinguish the

of experimental philosophy. The negative program aims at undermining some dominant methods and views in philosophy, such as the use of thought experiments; the positive program aims at understanding philosophically significant concepts as well as the psychology underlying philosophically significant judgments.
Experimental philosophy has been traditionally presented as studying
. It is, however, controversial what intuitions are in philosophy. Some propose that intuitions are a distinct kind of mental states, akin to perceptual states (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Chudnoff, E. (2013). Intuition. Oxford University Press. https://doi.org/10.1093/acprof:oso/9780199683000.001.0001

content.text: ); others identify them with (typically quick and unreflective) judgments that are not derived from philosophical theories, yet others, in a deflationary spirit, to any judgment applying a philosophical concept to a particular situation described by a short text (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Williamson T. (2007). The philosophy of philosophy. Blackwell. https://doi.org/10.1002/9780470696675

content.text: ). Some also deny that philosophy appeals to intuitions (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Cappelen, H. (2012). Philosophy without intuitions. Oxford University Press. https://doi.org/10.1093/acprof:oso/9780199644865.001.0001

content.text: Experimental philosophers working in the negative program often argue that their empirical findings challenge the
of philosophical intuitions or judgments (e.g., the Gettier intuition). They claim that these judgments are easily influenced by
(e.g., they manifest order effects) or that they are

The first question is metaphilosophical: To what extent does experimental philosophy challenge the way philosophy is conducted? Some philosophers have argued that experimental philosophers’ main findings challenge the theoretical ambitions of philosophers (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Machery, E. (2017). Philosophy within its proper bounds. Oxford University Press. https://doi.org/10.1093/oso/9780198807520.001.0001

content.text: ), others that they are mostly irrelevant (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Cappelen, H. (2012). Philosophy without intuitions. Oxford University Press. https://doi.org/10.1093/acprof:oso/9780199644865.001.0001
Deutsch, M. E. (2015). The myth of the intuitive: Experimental philosophy and philosophical method. MIT Press. https://doi.org/10.7551/mitpress/9780262028950.001.0001

content.text: A question that is both empirical and metaphilosophical is about the nature of expertise in philosophy: Do philosophers’ judgments and concepts differ from lay people’s? Are the former immune to the biases that influence the latter? Although research is still on-going, an impressive body of evidence suggests that philosophers and lay people’s concepts and judgments are more similar than one might have thought (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Schwitzgebel, E., & Cushman, F. (2015). Philosophers’ biased judgments persist despite training, expertise and reflection. Cognition, 141, 127–137. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2015.04.015

content.text: Other questions are more squarely empirical. Experimental philosophers disagree about the extent to which philosophically significant concepts and judgments vary, e.g., across cultures or social-economic status (for contrasting views, see

attrs.unstructuredValue: Knobe, J. (2019). Philosophical intuitions are surprisingly robust across demographic differences. Epistemology and the Philosophy of Science, 56(2), 29–36. https://doi.org/10.5840/eps201956225

Stich, S. P., & Machery, E. (2023). Demographic differences in philosophical intuition: A reply to Joshua Knobe. Review of Philosophy and Psychology, 14(2), 401–434. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13164-021-00609-7

content.text: Perhaps the most noteworthy development in experimental philosophy is the appeal to a more diverse set of tools and methods (e.g., reaction time measures, eye-tracking, qualitative interviews, ethnography; see

attrs.unstructuredValue: Fischer, E., & Curtis, M. (Eds.). (2019). Methodological advances in experimental philosophy. Bloomsbury Publishing. https://doi.org/10.5040/9781350069022

content.text: ). Particularly noteworthy is the use of text-analytic tools to study the use of concepts in an ecologically valid manner (e.g.,

attrs.unstructuredValue: Sytsma, J., & Reuter, K. (2017). Experimental philosophy of pain. Journal of Indian Council of Philosophical Research, 34, 611–628. https://doi.org/10.1007/s40961-017-0121-y

content.text: Finally, the replicability of experimental philosophy has been examined and appears to be substantial (

attrs.unstructuredValue: Cova, F., Strickland, B., Abatista, A., Allard, A., Andow, J., Attie, M., Beebe, J., Berniūnas, R., Boudesseul, J., Colombo, M., Cushman, F., Diaz, R., N’Djaye Nikolai van Dongen, N., Dranseika, V., Earp, B. D., Torres, A. G., Hannikainen, I., Hernández-Conde, J. V., Hu, W., . . . Zhou, X. (2021). Estimating the reproducibility of experimental philosophy. Review of Philosophy and Psychology, 12, 9–44. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13164-018-0400-9

content.text: Experimental philosophy has become a rich interdisciplinary research project that is intertwined with most areas of cognitive science, including anthropology, cultural psychology, moral psychology (e.g.,

attrs.unstructuredValue: Buckwalter, W., & Turri, J. (2015). Inability and obligation in moral judgment. PLoS One, 10(8), Article e0136589. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0136589
Icard, T. F., Kominsky, J. F., & Knobe, J. (2017). Normality and actual causal strength. Cognition, 161, 80–93. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2017.01.010

content.text: ), linguistics, and the cognitive science of religion.

Experimental philosophers’ work is influencing scientific research, particularly psychology, but also law, linguistics, and anthropology. Scientific journals regularly publish interdisciplinary work inspired by experimental philosophy, such as articles on the lay concepts of belief, religious credence, lying, consent, moral judgments about moral luck or about the reference of proper names, the relation between judgments about what is possible and moral judgments, causal judgments, or the nature of lexical statutory meaning, to give just a few examples.
Alfano, M., Machery, E., Plakias, A., & Loeb, D. (2022). Experimental moral philosophy. In E. N. Zalta & U. Nodelman (Eds.),
Knobe, J., & Nichols, N. (2017). Experimental philosophy. In E. N. Zalta (Ed.),
Li, J., & Zhu, X. (2023). Twenty years of experimental philosophy research.
The theory and practice of experimental philosophy
